nodes.name: Medieval History
nodes.description: Crusades, feudalism, Magna Carta, Black Death, and medieval Europe

items.prompt: Which Frankish king was crowned emperor by Pope Leo III on Christmas Day 800 CE and is known as the 'Father of Europe'?
items.explanation:
King of the Franks (768–814), King of the Lombards (774–814), Emperor (800–814)
Crowned by Pope Leo III at St. Peter’s Basilica on Christmas Day 800 CE
United most of Western and Central Europe — modern France, Germany, northern Italy, and Catalonia
Given the epithet Pater Europae (‘Father of Europe’) in his own lifetime
Launched the Carolingian Renaissance — promoted literacy, standardized script (Carolingian minuscule), and preserved classical texts
Forerunner to the line of Holy Roman Emperors, which persisted until 1806
Died 28 January 814; buried at Aachen Cathedral in modern-day Germany
items.cardFront: Crowned 800 CE, Pater Europae, Carolingian Renaissance

items.prompt: What medieval social and economic system structured society around relationships of land tenure, military service, and hierarchies of lords, vassals, and serfs?
items.explanation:
Dominated European social organization from roughly the 9th–15th centuries
King granted large estates (fiefs) to nobles in exchange for loyalty and military service
Vassals pledged homage to lords and provided armed knights; lords owed protection in return
Serfs were peasants legally bound to the manor — worked the land and surrendered a portion of harvest to the lord
Declined as commerce and coinage grew in the 13th century, allowing lords to pay money instead of military service
William the Conqueror imposed a particularly structured version on England after 1066
items.cardFront: Lords, vassals, serfs; land for military service

items.prompt: Which 1066 battle saw a Norman duke defeat Anglo-Saxon King Harold Godwinson, ending Anglo-Saxon rule in England and later inspiring a famous 70-metre embroidered tapestry?
items.explanation:
Fought 14 October 1066 between William, Duke of Normandy, and Anglo-Saxon King Harold Godwinson
Decisive Norman victory — Harold was killed (traditionally depicted with an arrow in his eye)
William crowned King of England on Christmas Day 1066 at Westminster Abbey
Bayeux Tapestry (c. 1067–1079), nearly 70 metres long, depicts the events in 58 scenes with 626 figures
Triggered by succession crisis after the death of childless King Edward the Confessor in January 1066
William crossed the English Channel with ~8,000 men and 5,000 horses, landing at Pevensey on 28 September

items.prompt: What series of religious wars launched by the papacy between 1096 and 1291 aimed to recover the Holy Land from Muslim control?
items.explanation:
Series of military campaigns (1096–1291) proclaimed by the papacy to recapture Jerusalem and the Holy Land
First called by Pope Urban II at the Council of Clermont in November 1095
First expedition (1096–1099) captured Jerusalem and established four Crusader states in the Levant
Key leaders of the First included Godfrey of Bouillon, Bohemond of Taranto, and Raymond of Toulouse
Saladin recaptured Jerusalem in 1187, prompting the Third led by Richard the Lionheart
Ended with the fall of Acre in 1291, the last major Crusader stronghold on the mainland
At least nine major expeditions over nearly two centuries; Children’s expedition (1212) never reached the Holy Land

items.prompt: Which 1215 document, sealed at Runnymede under pressure from rebellious barons, established that the English king was not above the law?
items.explanation:
Sealed by King John at Runnymede on 15 June 1215 — a meadow between Windsor Castle and the rebel base at Staines
Forced on John by rebellious barons; mediated by Archbishop Stephen Langton
Established principle that the sovereign is subject to the rule of law
Promised protection of church rights, limits on feudal payments, access to swift justice, and a council of 25 barons
Annulled by Pope Innocent III within months, triggering the First Barons’ War
Revised and reissued multiple times; became a foundational document of English constitutional liberty
Influenced the U.S. Bill of Rights and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights
items.cardFront: 1215, Runnymede, King John, foundation of English liberty

items.prompt: Which 14th-century pandemic, caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, killed an estimated 30–60% of Europe’s population between 1347 and 1351?
items.explanation:
Arrived in Europe in 1347 via Genoese traders fleeing Kaffa in Crimea; spread by fleas on black rats
Killed an estimated 25–50 million Europeans — roughly 30–60% of the population
Caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis; manifested as bubonic, pneumonic, and septicemic plague
Florence’s population fell from ~120,000 to 50,000; half of Paris’s 100,000 inhabitants died
Labor shortages after the plague weakened serfdom and shifted economic power toward surviving peasants
Second great natural disaster of the Late Middle Ages, after the Great Famine of 1315–1317

items.prompt: Which intermittent conflict between England and France, lasting from 1337 to 1453, featured the battles of Crécy, Poitiers, and Agincourt?
items.explanation:
Fought 1337–1453 (116 years) between England and France over feudal rights and the French crown
Triggered by Edward III of England’s claim to the French throne through his mother Isabella
Three phases: Edwardian War (1337–1360), Caroline War (1369–1389), Lancastrian War (1415–1453)
Key English victories: Crécy (1346), Poitiers (1356, Black Prince captured French King John II), Agincourt (1415)
Turning point: Joan of Arc lifted the siege of Orléans in 1429, reviving French fortunes
France ultimately won; England lost all continental possessions except Calais (held until 1558)
Interrupted by the Black Death and punctuated by long truces

items.prompt: Which teenage peasant girl claimed divine visions, led the French army to lift the siege of Orléans in 1429, was burned for heresy at 19, and was canonized in 1920?
items.explanation:
Born c. 1412 in Domrémy, France; claimed visions from Saints Michael, Catherine, and Margaret
Led the French army to break the English siege of Orléans in May 1429 — a turning point in the Hundred Years’ War
Captured by Burgundians in 1430 and sold to the English
Tried for heresy by Bishop Pierre Cauchon; charges included wearing men’s clothing and acting on demonic visions
Burned at the stake in Rouen on 30 May 1431, aged about 19
Verdict overturned in 1456; beatified 1909 by Pius X; canonized 16 May 1920 by Benedict XV
Only person burned at the stake for heresy who later became a Catholic saint

items.prompt: Which loose confederation of Central European territories, famously described by Voltaire as 'neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire,' lasted from 962 to 1806?
items.explanation:
Founded in 962 when Otto the Great was crowned emperor; dissolved in 1806 by Napoleon
Heartland in Germany; at various times included modern Germany, Austria, Czech Republic, Netherlands, and northern Italy
Voltaire’s quip from Essay on Universal History (1756): ‘neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire’
Power fragmented among hundreds of principalities, especially after the Thirty Years’ War (1618–1648)
Emperor elected by seven (later more) prince-electors — not a hereditary monarchy
Charlemagne’s Carolingian Empire (crowned 800) was the spiritual predecessor
Habsburg dynasty held the title almost continuously from 1438 until dissolution

items.prompt: Which empire, the eastern continuation of Rome with its capital at Constantinople, endured for nearly 1,000 years after the Western Roman Empire fell and was finally conquered by the Ottomans in 1453?
items.explanation:
Eastern continuation of the Roman Empire; capital at Constantinople (modern Istanbul)
Survived the fall of the Western Roman Empire (476 CE) by nearly 1,000 years
Fell on 29 May 1453 when Sultan Mehmed II (‘the Conqueror’), age 21, breached the walls with massive cannons
Last emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos died defending the walls; defenders numbered only ~7,000 against ~120,000 Ottomans
Preserved Greco-Roman law, literature, and learning; Justinian’s Code (529–534) codified Roman law
Hagia Sophia (537 CE) was the world’s largest cathedral for nearly 1,000 years
Fall triggered an exodus of Greek scholars to Italy, fueling the Italian Renaissance

items.prompt: Which Norse seafaring people from Scandinavia raided, traded, and settled across Europe from 793 to 1066, with their age traditionally beginning at the sack of Lindisfarne?
items.explanation:
Viking Age conventionally dated 793–1066 — from the raid on Lindisfarne monastery to the Norman Conquest
Lindisfarne (8 June 793): monks killed or enslaved, monastery plundered — shocked Christendom
Originated from Denmark, Norway, and Sweden; used advanced longships for speed and shallow-water navigation
Leif Erikson (c. 1000 CE) reached North America (‘Vinland’) — roughly 500 years before Columbus
Established the Danelaw in England, founded Normandy (Rollo, 911), and the Kievan Rus’ in Eastern Europe
Both William the Conqueror and Harald Hardrada who invaded England in 1066 had Viking ancestry
Traded from Baghdad to Newfoundland; served as Varangian Guard to the Byzantine emperor

items.prompt: Which empire, founded by Temujin in 1206, became the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from the Pacific to Eastern Europe?
items.explanation:
Founded in 1206 when Temujin was proclaimed Genghis Khan (‘universal ruler’) by a council of Mongol tribes
Largest contiguous land empire in history — ~24 million km², roughly 17% of Earth’s landmass at its peak
Genghis Khan (c. 1162–1227) conquered from the Pacific to the Caspian Sea in his lifetime
Enforced the Pax Mongolica — stabilized Silk Road trade routes, enabling exchange between Europe and China
Destroyed Baghdad in 1258, ending the Abbasid Caliphate; killed an estimated 40 million people total
Split into four khanates after succession disputes: Golden Horde, Chagatai, Ilkhanate, and Yuan (China)
Genghis Khan’s burial site remains unknown; a 2003 study estimated ~0.5% of the world’s male population carries a Y-chromosome lineage attributed to him, though recent genomic studies have questioned this link
items.cardFront: Genghis Khan, 1206, largest contiguous land empire

items.prompt: What institution, established by Pope Gregory IX in 1231 to combat heresy, later evolved into a notorious Spanish version authorized in 1478 under Ferdinand and Isabella?
items.explanation:
Medieval version established 1231 by Pope Gregory IX; assigned Dominican friars to try heretics in southern France and Italy
Pope Innocent IV authorized the use of torture against heretics in 1252 (bull Ad extirpanda)
Spanish version authorized 1478 by Pope Sixtus IV at the request of Ferdinand and Isabella
Tomás de Torquemada became first Inquisitor-General of Spain in 1483 — burned ~2,000 heretics at the stake
Spanish version initially targeted conversos (Jewish converts) suspected of secretly practicing Judaism
Spanish version not abolished until 1834 — lasted over 350 years
Roman Inquisition (1542) later tried Galileo for heliocentrism in 1633

items.prompt: Which medieval commercial and defensive alliance of merchant guilds, centered on Lübeck, dominated trade across Northern Europe from the 13th to 17th centuries?
items.explanation:
Commercial and defensive network of merchant guilds and market towns in Central and Northern Europe
Grew from Lübeck (‘Queen of the Hansa’) in the late 12th century; peaked 13th–15th centuries
Encompassed nearly 200 settlements across eight modern countries, from Estonia to the Netherlands
Four major trading posts (kontors): Novgorod, London, Bruges, and Bergen
Traded furs, timber, wax, and grain from the east for cloth and manufactured goods from the west
Controlled trade routes from the Baltic to the North Sea for over 400 years
Declined in the 17th century as nation-states consolidated power and new Atlantic trade routes emerged

items.prompt: Which catastrophic agricultural crisis of 1315–1317, caused by extreme rainfall and crop failure, killed an estimated 10–25% of Northern Europe’s population and preceded an even deadlier pandemic?
items.explanation:
Struck Northern Europe 1315–1317; affected the British Isles, France, Low Countries, Scandinavia, Germany, and western Poland
Caused by unusually heavy rain beginning spring 1315 — crops could not ripen, leading to widespread failure
Estimated 10–25% of the urban population died; Europe did not fully recover until 1322
Great Bovine Pestilence killed up to 80% of sheep and cattle, compounding food shortages
Period marked by extreme crime, disease, and reports of cannibalism and infanticide
First great natural disaster of the Late Middle Ages — preceded the Black Death (1347–1351) by 30 years
items.cardFront: 1315–1317, crop failure, preceded the Black Death
